titleInfo.title: Verses from on High: The Ascent of T’ai Shan

mods.abstract:
Samples of pre-Tang verses on Mount Tai: Lu Ji 陸機 (261–303): A Chant of Mount Tai Xie Lingyun 謝靈運 (385–433): Chant of Mount Tai the poetess Xie Daoyun 謝道韞 (Schwiegertochter von Wang Xizhi): Ascending the Mountain Du Fu 杜甫(712–770): Gazing afar at the Marchmount, written on a private excursion Lu Zhaolin 盧照鄰 (ca. 635–684) accompanied Tang Gaozong in 666: Songs for the Great Refection upon the Ascent for the feng. Zhang Yue 張說 (967–731) for Xuanzong in 725, composed 14 solemn songs for the rites, and an altar hymn for the feng sacrifice, Tang Xuanzong bestowed titles to Mount Tai and other mountains and landmarks in his empire. Cao Zhi 曹植 (192–232)： Combines in his poem Mount Tai as protector of the state with a place for Daoist transformation, i.e. Huangdi obtaining wings and becoming a transcendent The latter’s father, Cao Cao 曹操 (155–220): Three Cantos on the Pneuma Emerging, had already written on Mount Tai as a place where transcendents descend to earth
Li Bo 李白 (701–762): Six poems, entitled Wandering on Mount Tai, in which he becomes a Daoist adept and finally, at night on the summit, turns into a transcendent, but just for one night. Only two gentlemen of the Way seem to have been remembered for their association with Mount Tai: Xu Jun 許峻 (early second century), a master of divinatory practices, and Zhang Zhong 張忠 (first half of the fourth century), an expert in Daoist respiratory techniques, whom Zhu Senglang 竺僧朗 (ca. 315–400) follwed into the mountain in 351.
